original_reasoning: ستيك under tā chapter; steko = to stand. Likely the root س ت/ك meaning 'stand fixed'. Phonetically and semantically aligned. Codex undervalued. Calibration: clear cognate.

الأفعاء: السمة التي تكون على صورة الأفعى (a brand/mark in the shape of a snake). γλύφω=to carve, engrave, cut out. A snake-shaped brand IS a carved/engraved mark. Both involve incised marks on surfaces. Strong connection through marking/engraving.
Plausible mineral-domain overlap: Arabic includes soft stones; Greek denotes antimony or kohl.
Arabic 'hairy' can plausibly meet Greek 'gourd' through the fuzzy exterior of gourds.

البسل = آمين (amen, solemn affirmation). εὐλαβής = holding fast, clinging to with reverence/caution. Both occupy the semantic field of solemn careful assent and devout holding. The one who says amen holds firmly to the prayer -- strong convergence.
Arabic nearness/reduced distance plausibly drifts toward smallness, hence a diminutive notion.
the water-carrier is one who has/holds water, so 'hold/have' is a plausible direct extension.
Arabic فحيح = 'hissing (of snake)'; Greek = 'serpent-handler'. Both involve snakes; conceptually linked though not direct match.
